entries.title: Urbit
entries.summary: Get on the network in seconds through a hosting provider, or run Urbit yourself
entries.description: Building Urbit, the computer designed to last forever

entries.title: Running your Urbit
entries.summary: Explore different ways to run your own Urbit, from Layer 1 to Layer 2 identities, cloud hosting to local self-hosting, with options for both developers and end users.

entries.title: Cloud Server
entries.summary: Run Urbit on a VPS from cloud providers like Digital Ocean or Hetzner for reliable uptime, remote access, and professional infrastructure while maintaining full data control.
entries.description: Running in a virtual private server (VPS) affords easy solutions to things like DNS and remote access, at a marginal cost to tangible control of your data

entries.title: Command Line
entries.summary: Run Urbit from the Unix command line by downloading the runtime binary, obtaining a planet or comet identity, and booting with simple terminal commands.
entries.description: Every Urbit OS server is made unique by its Urbit ID, which others can use to reach you on the network. There are five ranks of Urbit ID, but the one an ordinary user needs is a planet, which has a four-syllable name like "~sampel-palnet". Unless you know someone who can gift you one, or you want to get one from hosting provider like Tlon, you'll need to buy one.

entries.title: Native Planet
entries.summary: Native Planet sells specialized hardware with Groundseg preinstalled for streamlined Urbit hosting, combining local control with optional Startram DNS and remote access services.
entries.description: Native Planet builds hardware and software for simplified home hosting of your Urbit

entries.title: Tlon Hosting
entries.summary: Tlon Corporation offers free managed hosting with quick onboarding via phone or email signup, ideal for users wanting Tlon Messenger without handling self-hosting.
entries.description: Tlon Corporation is the preeminent hosting provider which provides free and seamless onboarding to the Urbit network

entries.title: Go Deeper
entries.summary: Deepen your Urbit knowledge with resources on architecture, identity systems, applications, and the community building the future of personal computing.
entries.description: Ready to explore more? Learn about Urbit's architecture, identity system, and the broader ecosystem of applications and tools being built on the network.

entries.title: Hosting Providers
entries.summary: Third-party hosting providers run your Urbit while you retain ownership, offering a managed alternative to self-hosting for users who prefer not to handle maintenance.
entries.description: While urbit is designed to be run by it's users, and so simple that caring for it would be as simple as caring for a cactus, it's not quite there yet. And some people aren't inclined to want to take on the burden of learning how to run their own urbit. If this sound like you, don't worry. Third party hosting providers can run your urbit on your behalf, while still maintaining many of the ownership characteristics that make urbit yours (in stark contrast to legacy cloud software.

entries.title: Self-Hosting
entries.summary: Self-hosting your Urbit gives you full control over your personal server, balancing complete sovereignty with the responsibility of maintaining your own instance.
entries.description: Urbit is an attempt to build a computer that is truly yours, designed to last a lifetime, with which you can form trustworthy networks free of extractive middlemen. Part of that means a being a networked computer that can be run by it's users. That said, Urbit is still under active development, so self-hosting is currently most apt for users who aren't afraid of a little bit of tinkering. If that's not you, we recommend using a hosting provider instead.

entries.title: Get Started with Urbit
entries.summary: Begin your Urbit journey by acquiring a self-sovereign ID and learning about Urbit OS, the personal server computing platform that gives you full control over your data.
entries.description: Ready to join the Urbit network? Get your own Urbit ID and start exploring a new way to compute.

entries.title: Blog
entries.summary: Latest updates, developer spotlights, and technical deep dives from the Urbit community.
entries.description: Latest updates, developer spotlights, and technical deep dives from the Urbit community.

entries.title: ~2019.10 Roadmap
entries.summary: Mid-year progress update on Urbit development, featuring Bridge public release, improved sigils, Arvo updates, Landscape progress, and plans for network explorer and mobile support.
entries.description: Galen Wolfe-Pauly on the road ahead for the identity/OS/interface/community stack.

entries.title: ~2019.5 Roadmap
entries.summary: Overview of Urbit development progress and roadmap through Q3 2019, covering Azimuth improvements, Arvo updates, Bridge redesign, Hoon School launch, and the grants program.
entries.description: Where we are and where we're going as of mid-2019.

entries.summary: Year-end reflection on 2020 achievements including OS 1 adoption, Ford Fusion stability improvements, hosting service launch, and network reset with data preservation, plus 2021 priorities for developer community growth.
entries.description: Reflecting and looking forward.

entries.title: Late 2020 Progress Update: OS 1 -> OS 1.N
entries.summary: Six-month update on Urbit development focusing on 10x performance gains, memory optimization, new build system replacing Ford Fusion, graph store integration, and stability improvements to infrastructure and interface.
entries.description: When we announced OS 1, in April, we started to disappear into Urbit. Since then, we’ve been living on Urbit like we never have before.

entries.title: Models of Society
entries.summary: Examination of how digital conversation forms shape society, arguing for Urbit's territory-based model where users own persistent identities and can build distinct digital spaces without centralized platform control.
entries.description: Conversations compose society. What composes conversation — how do we digitize it in a way that enhances society without imposing upon it? How do we form this new medium, both to facilitate natural human behavior and to inspire the best of it?

entries.title: Report from the field: Assembly 2021
entries.summary: Recap of Urbit Assembly 2021 conference in Austin, highlighting the community's new world energy, hardware and software project announcements, and the relationship between system building and community formation.
entries.description: The system builds the community and the community builds the system.

entries.title: The Promise and Paradox of Decentralization
entries.summary: Analysis of centralization trends in decentralized networks, examining the decentralization sandwich phenomenon, onramp privatization, and Urbit's approach to balancing identity ownership with open protocols.
entries.description: Is centralization just a natural tendency of all networks? Are we destined to have a 'decentralization sandwich?'

entries.title: A New Epoch for The Forever Computer
entries.summary: Announcement of Urbit Foundation governance transition with new three-seat board, appointment of Executive Director ~sicdev-pilnup, and evolution toward operational decentralization and broader community amplification.
entries.description: On the further decentralization of Urbit and the next era of the Urbit Foundation

entries.title: A Founder's Farewell
entries.summary: Curtis Yarvin's resignation from Tlon as CTO, board member, and voting shareholder, describing his 17-year journey building Urbit from 2002-2013 and explaining his goal of creating an open technology not controlled by any single person.
entries.description: My goal was always to fire myself at the first possible opportunity. I'm super happy to reach it.

entries.title: A Topiary: Hypertext and Urbit
entries.summary: Historical overview of hypertext from Vannevar Bush's Memex through Ted Nelson's Project Xanadu to the modern web, explaining Urbit's %graph-store data structure as a peer-to-peer alternative to proprietary platform graph databases.
entries.description: A brief history of hypertext and Urbit networking

entries.title: Aesthetic Culture #1
entries.summary: First in monthly Urbit art digest series showcasing community-created digital art, including wooden sigil sculptures with lighting effects, Dinosaur Comics threads using urbit.org as seed text, and cat poetry.
entries.description: One of the most exciting things about Urbit is the aesthetic and design around it, developed partly by Tlon (through the design of Urbit itself) and partly by the community (by producing great Urbit art).

entries.title: After the Machine War
entries.summary: Speculative fiction set in 2050 New York City depicting a dystopian future where iThink microchips deliver drug-induced compliance, surveillance is pervasive, and content moderation is a mundane job in a world stripped of natural experiences.
entries.description: The date is January 1, 2050. The place, New York City. The vibe...subdued.

entries.title: The Dream of the Agency DAO
entries.summary: Examination of creative agency DAOs as potential solutions to modern advertising inefficiencies, exploring project bountification, talent retention through token ownership, and idea valuation while acknowledging challenges of creative work by committee.
entries.description: As creative studios and agencies struggle for more creative freedom, DAOs and tokenization will surely take a more central role in the marketing and branding industries. Decentralization offers such entities benefits that could fundamentally reshape creatives’ relationships with clients—reducing layers of inefficiency and anti-creative incentives.

entries.title: An Email from the Archive
entries.summary: Forwarded 2020 email from Galen Wolfe-Pauly discussing Urbit's principles of simplicity, durability, and ownership in digital communication tools, arguing that platform-centric software creates fundamental user conflicts.
entries.description: I found this email in my archives recently and thought it might be fun to share publicly.

entries.title: An Urbit Overview
entries.summary: Introduction to Urbit as a virtual city of general-purpose personal servers, describing technical architecture including Nock formal semantics, encrypted peer-to-peer networking, cryptographic identity, and advantages over fragmented cloud services.
entries.description: A high-level overview of Urbit.

entries.title: Announcing: Urbit Grants Program
entries.summary: Launch of Urbit Grants program providing Azimuth star compensation through three mechanisms: semi-annual gifts for informal contributions, bounties for defined projects, and proposals for contributor-pitched ideas.
entries.description: Announcing Urbit Grants, a way to earn stars through contributing.

entries.title: Ares
entries.summary: Technical description of Ares runtime improvements including subject knowledge analysis for Nock codegen, 2stackz noun allocator eliminating heap, and Persistent Memory Arena enabling terabyte-scale data management through single-level store optimization.
entries.description: A light technical description of Ares, the new Urbit runtime

entries.title: August Grants Program Review
entries.summary: Article examining grants distribution.
entries.description: The completion of the first cohort of Hoon School Live and the following App School Live program minted dozens of capable new Hoon developers. These developers are completing applications, closing out bounties, and putting together proposals at a rapid pace, with more to come as Assembly 2022 draws near.

entries.title: Azimuth as Multipass
entries.summary: Essay envisioning Azimuth as a unified identity and payment system—a 'civilizational key' serving as both driver's license and credit card for a decentralized digital society.
entries.description: What if everyone had a single 'civilizational key'?

entries.title: Azimuth is On-Chain
entries.summary: Announcement of Urbit address space deployment to Ethereum blockchain as Azimuth, introducing Bridge interface for managing ERC-721 identity tokens and launching Landscape cities for private communities.
entries.description: The Urbit address space, now called Azimuth, is on the blockchain. And too many other things to fit into a single post.

entries.title: Azimuth Security Bounty Program
entries.summary: Launch of security bounty program incentivizing responsible disclosure of Azimuth smart contract vulnerabilities, specifying reward tiers based on severity and process for submission and verification.
entries.description: Inviting you (and your friends) to help us make Azimuth as secure as possible.

entries.title: Beliefs and Principles Guiding the Urbit Project
entries.summary: Foundational principles for Urbit governance including digital independence, code-as-law, decentralized control, stake-based governance with equal authority per stake, and content-neutral moderation.
entries.description: We believe.

entries.title: Bootstrapping Urbit from Ethereum
entries.summary: Technical analysis of leveraging Ethereum for Urbit's identity layer while maintaining independence, discussing smart contract security, gas economics, and long-term decentralization strategy.
entries.description: We've decided to launch Urbit's constitution as a system of Ethereum contracts.

entries.title: Building 'Beyond Beginner Guitar' on Urbit
entries.summary: ~nordus-mocwyl details building an Urbit-powered guitar course business today with YouTube discovery, email communication, and Stripe payments, while dreaming of a fully decentralized flow with LLM recommendations, cryptocurrency payments, and native course syncing.
entries.description: ~nordus-mocwyl walks through what it took to build a guitar course and member community on Urbit

entries.title: Common Objections to Urbit
entries.summary: Response to frequent criticisms of Urbit including complexity concerns, adoption challenges, and decentralization tradeoffs, providing technical context and community perspectives on each objection.
entries.description: Some common objections to Urbit, discussed.

entries.title: Community Spotlight: The Portico
entries.summary: Feature on community-governed social network application running on Urbit, demonstrating Landscape's app distribution model and explaining governance structure using Urbit IDs and star-based voting.
entries.description: Interview with The Portico founder Josh Reagan

entries.title: Contributor Spotlight: ~dozreg-toplud
entries.summary: UrWASM and SKA developer ~dozreg-toplud discusses Urbit's shallow stack, security model, and work on improving Nock performance through subject knowledge analysis.
entries.description: A peek into the mind behind UrWASM, and the undertaking to make Urbit faster

entries.title: Contributor Spotlight: ~litneb-maltyp
entries.summary: Designer ~litneb-maltyp discusses Urbit's identity system, owning personal servers, and building a home lab for agency over computing and digital permanence.
entries.description: A conversation with ~litneb-maltyp on Urbit identity, community, and the personal server

entries.title: Contributor Spotlight: ~mastyr-bottec
entries.summary: Runtime developer ~mastyr-bottec explains vere64 work enabling Urbit to store large amounts of data, including migrations and lifting file size limits.
entries.description: A conversation with runtime developer ~mastyr-bottec on his work to make Urbit store large amounts of data

entries.title: Contributor Spotlight: ~bonbud-macryg & ~niblyx-malnus
entries.summary: Groundwire developers ~bonbud-macryg and ~niblyx-malnus explore Urbit for AI, building LLM tools like Clurd and MCP servers, and Urbit as a trustworthy personal computing platform.
entries.description: A conversation with Groundwire developers ~bonbud-macryg and ~niblyx-malnus on Urbit, AI, and building beautiful computers

entries.title: Contributor Spotlight: ~nomryg-nilref
entries.summary: ~nomryg-nilref discusses how Urbit's sovereign computing vision resonated with decades of technical experience, why simple software distribution matters, and what Obelisk is trying to fix in relational databases.
entries.description: A conversation with ~nomryg-nilref on beautiful lost causes, forever software, and Obelisk

entries.title: Contributor Spotlight: ~nordus-mocwyl
entries.summary: Musician ~nordus-mocwyl discusses using Urbit for independent music distribution, building guitar courses with Hawk, and learning Hoon to understand computing from Nock upward.
entries.description: A conversation with ~nordus-mocwyl on independent music, Urbit, and direct charity

entries.title: Contributor Spotlight: ~palfun-foslup
entries.summary: Tlon engineer ~palfun-foslup discusses building Gall apps like %pals and %rumors, why user-owned servers lower the barrier for peer-to-peer software, and what Gall 2026 aims to deliver with userspace compatibility and permissions.
entries.description: A conversation with ~palfun-foslup on Gall, userspace, and making simple software

entries.title: Convivial Networks
entries.summary: Essay on designing network protocols supporting human-scale interaction, arguing for technical architectures that enable communities to self-govern rather than imposing centralized moderation.
entries.description: Like the relationships that we build within them, our platforms should yield satisfaction precisely because they’re non-trivial; they demand effort, which is another way of saying they require engagement with the world.

entries.title: Creating Sigils
entries.summary: Technical deep-dive into sigil generation algorithm, explaining glyph encoding, color assignment logic, collision avoidance techniques, and principles for visual identity in digital networks.
entries.description: The origin and design process informing Urbit's generative user avatar system, Sigils.

entries.title: Desire Lines to a New Internet
entries.summary: The author describes web3 communities migrating to Urbit as traversing Christopher Alexander's desire lines, with Layer 2 making planet distribution affordable, DAO tooling proliferating on Landscape, and token-gated distributions scaling through projects like Dusko and PointDAO.
entries.description: As more DAOs, NFT and digital communities find their way to Urbit, others are likely to follow their paths, making them their own, just like the network itself.

entries.title: Developer Preview: vere64 runtime
entries.summary: The Urbit Foundation releases a vere64 Developer Preview enabling terabyte-scale looms through 64-bit runtime, removing the conceptual 16GB glass ceiling for experimental applications while warning it's for development ships only without migration pathways.
entries.description: A preview for developers to experience an unlimited loom using the vere64 runtime

entries.title: Eliza
entries.summary: Introduction to AI assistant running on Urbit architecture, demonstrating how personal servers enable private, owned implementations of conversational interfaces without third-party data collection.
entries.description: Building things, even Calm™ things, makes noise.

entries.title: Urbit Events Series
entries.summary: Lane Rettig launches Urbit's community event series with developer calls, a December Town Hall, and upcoming UrbitCon, designed to scale cultural knowledge transmission, document maker expertise, and foster community-led growth as the project reaches critical mass.
entries.description: These events are an opportunity for Urbit contributors to share real-time updates that don’t make it into this blog, and for the community to get to know the contributors (and one another).

entries.title: Azimuth’s First Contract Upgrade
entries.summary: Jonathan Paprocki and Mark explain Azimuth's first Galactic Senate upgrade vote on ERC721 fixes, self-modifying proxies, and Claims contract improvements while reviewing Urbit's decentralization journey from Tlon-controlled to galaxy-owner governed.
entries.description: Galactic Senate makes first concrete action

entries.title: First Steps Towards urbit.org
entries.summary: Josh Lehman announces urbit.org's separation from Tlon as a protocol steward, outlining plans for developer recruitment, grants acceleration, bounties for Landscape development, and community-driven address space distribution to mature the platform.
entries.description: With a stable platform taking shape and a strong community forming that wants to help build Urbit, it’s time to make urbit.org real.

entries.title: Ford Fusion
entries.summary: Technical explanation of build system simplification eliminating traditional compilation, replacing it with filesystem-based deployment and explaining performance and reliability improvements.
entries.description: Ford Fusion was an overhaul of Urbit's over-the-air upgrade process and a rewrite of its build system. The new update system corrects a few long-standing bugs with the previous one, and the new build system is simpler, smaller (by around 5,000 lines), and easier to manage.

entries.title: Urbit's Open Source Culture, Part I
entries.summary: N E Davis traces Urbit's evolution from Curtis Yarvin's personal project through Tlon's skunkworks to today's thriving open source community, crediting nine factors from functional OS appeal to public build parties in cultivating radical transparency.
entries.description: How did Urbit cultivate a unique open source software culture? Let's take a look at how we got to where we are today.

entries.title: Urbit's Open Source Culture, Part II
entries.summary: N E Davis proposes four priorities for Urbit's open source future: developing Hoon Prime for legible code, improving developer experience with better tooling, highlighting user stories, and maintaining playfulness while building toward mature documentation.
entries.description: How will Urbit continue to foster its innovative open source software culture?

entries.title: The Future of Userspace
entries.summary: An overview of the Gall 2026 userspace roadmap, including scoped Eyre permissions, support for alternative agent APIs, and a kelvin-shimming proof of concept for application compatibility across Arvo updates.
entries.description: The Gall 2026 project and what it means for userspace

entries.title: Gifts Q3 2020
entries.summary: Quarterly grants distribution announcement recognizing technical contributions, documentation improvements, community building efforts, and ecosystem development work across Urbit's open source projects.
entries.description: Twice a year we distribute address space to those that have made valuable contributions to Urbit. Now called our Gifts program, the gifting of address space has been part of Urbit long before we had a grants program.

entries.title: Governance of urbit.org
entries.summary: Article examining governance.
entries.description: Stewardship of the Urbit Project.

entries.title: Assembly Hackathon 2023
entries.summary: Article examining community event.
entries.description: The Assembly 2023 Hackathon was the most successful Urbit Hackathon we've had. Get a taste of Demo Day in Lisbon and check out the projects they made.

entries.title: Hackathon Results
entries.summary: Award announcements detailing winning projects across categories, judge evaluations, prize distributions, and selected submissions demonstrating landscape development progress.
entries.description: We recently held an invite-only Urbit Hackathon for graduates of our Hoon School program, and the submissions really impressed us across the board. Submissions were judged on several criteria: creativity, usefulness, and code quality.

entries.title: NFTs, Urbit IDs, and Communities w/ Haleek Maul
entries.summary: Matt interviews Haleek Maul about Holdersland's Urbit NFT project, exploring how blockchain lowers barriers for Caribbean artists, enables peer-to-peer communities, and creates new financial structures beyond traditional music industry gatekeepers.
entries.description: An interview with the founder of Holdersland

entries.title: A Perspective on Lisp and Hoon
entries.summary: N E Davis compares Hoon to Lisp across syntax, metaprogramming, and philosophy, showing how Hoon's static typing, binary tree homoiconicity, and subject-oriented design advance Lisp's goals for a hundred-year computer while avoiding macro proliferation.
entries.description: Lisp is an éminence grise of programming.  How does Hoon compare?

entries.title: Hosting the Future
entries.summary: Discussion of Urbit hosting infrastructure options including commercial providers, self-hosting guides, redundancy strategies, and long-term continuity planning for personal servers.
entries.description: The way we see it, hosting is the most important thing, next to Landscape, that Tlon can do to help Urbit continue toward widespread adoption.

entries.title: Immunology for the Internet Age
entries.summary: The author draws an immunology metaphor for the Internet's evolution from high-trust academia to megacorp-dominated zero-trust systems, arguing DAOs, blockchain identity, and decentralized platforms form an immune system preserving human agency against corporate colonization.
entries.description: A consideration of the history of the Internet motivates introspection on the nature and causes of social dysfunction in a globally shared space. Centralized solutions fail to yield satisfactory outcomes for human freedom and thriving. Decentralized autonomous organizations and their technological apparatus together represent the evolution of an immune system against a corporatized Internet.

entries.title: Infrastructural
entries.summary: Technical essay examining network infrastructure requirements, explaining choices supporting long-term durability, decentralization, and resistance to single points of failure.
entries.description: A reflection–meditation on OS 1’s initial form development, and the attitude we brought to bear in designing it.

entries.title: Interim Constitution
entries.summary: Governance document establishing temporary constitutional framework for Urbit Foundation during transition period, defining board powers, galactic senate responsibilities, and amendment processes.
entries.description: The governing rules for the early days of the Urbit network.

entries.title: Interplanetary Commerce
entries.summary: Speculative essay on economic systems in distributed networks, arguing that Urbit's ownership model enables direct peer-to-peer commerce without intermediary platforms taking transaction fees.
entries.description: OS-level commercial primitives.

entries.title: Introducing OS 1
entries.summary: Launch announcement for Urbit operating system upgrade featuring Landscape interface, graph store data structure, improved network performance, and expanded application ecosystem.
entries.description: OS 1 is somewhere between ‘productivity software’ and a ‘social network’. We think it’s the beginning of an altogether new breed of social computing.

entries.title: Introduction to the Combine DAO
entries.summary: Whitepaper describing DAO governance architecture implementing bicameral decision-making, combining star-based voting with identity verification for decentralized organizational coordination.
entries.description: Inside the mind of the Combine

entries.title: Input and Output in Hoon
entries.summary: Philip Monk compares imperative, monadic, and state machine IO paradigms, defending Urbit's 90% state machine approach for permanence and robustness, with monadic threads for complex IO sequences that don't require upgradeability.
entries.description: Let's talk about IO in Urbit.

entries.title: Lunar Urbit and the Internet of Things
entries.summary: Jonathan Paprocki argues Urbit moons solve IoT's privacy, security, and vendor lock-in problems through self-authenticating identities, deterministic state, source-independent packet routing, and peer-to-peer data markets for industrial applications like agriculture.
entries.description: Potential future use cases of moons for industry and consumers

entries.title: Landscape: A Portrait
entries.summary: Visual and technical introduction to Landscape interface design, explaining component architecture, interaction patterns, and principles guiding user experience development.
entries.description: On the latest Urbit user interface, and the interfaces to come.

entries.title: Layer 2 FAQ
entries.summary: Frequently asked questions addressing Urbit's relationship to blockchain layer 2 solutions, clarifying architectural distinctions and explaining why Urbit doesn't require additional scaling layers.
entries.description: Answers to all your lingering L2 questions.

entries.title: LLMs on Urbit
entries.summary: Analysis of running large language models on personal servers, discussing privacy advantages, local inference options, and architectures for combining AI capabilities with owned infrastructure.
entries.description: A walkthrough of how to use current Urbit LLM tooling by ~niblyx-malnus

entries.title: Magic
entries.summary: Philosophical essay on technology's role in human experience, arguing for systems that augment rather than replace natural capabilities, and examining aesthetics of computing interfaces.
entries.description: A thought-experiment to explain the Urbit user experience.

entries.title: Metaphase
entries.summary: Vision statement describing Urbit's development methodology, arguing for iterative evolution through actual use rather than theoretical design, and explaining community-driven improvement cycles.
entries.description: On the upcoming and foregoing Landscape lifecycles, and other forms of mitosis across the Urbit project.

entries.title: Nockmas 2025: Day 1
entries.summary: Covers opcode 0 (Address), the fas slot operator for navigating Nock nouns as binary trees using even numbers for left branches and odd numbers for right branches.
entries.description: 12 days of Nockmas: Address, opcode 0

entries.title: Nockmas 2025: Day 10
entries.summary: Covers opcode 9 (Invoke), evaluating a core from formula c then executing the arm at address b within that core with the core itself as subject.
entries.description: 12 days of Nockmas: Invoke, opcode 9

entries.title: Nockmas 2025: Day 11
entries.summary: Covers opcode 10 (Edit), the hax # operator replacing a noun at a given address within a structure with a new value, used for gate calls and memory optimization.
entries.description: 12 days of Nockmas: Edit, opcode 10

entries.title: Nockmas 2025: Day 12
entries.summary: Covers opcode 11 (Hint), attaching metadata to Nock expressions without affecting evaluation, supporting static tags and dynamic hints for runtime optimizations.
entries.description: 12 days of Nockmas: Hint, opcode 11

entries.title: Nockmas 2025: Day 2
entries.summary: Covers opcode 1 (Constant), which returns its argument b while ignoring the subject, used for storing data and executable Nock expressions for later evaluation.
entries.description: 12 days of Nockmas: Constant, opcode 1

entries.title: Nockmas 2025: Day 3
entries.summary: Covers opcode 2 (Evaluate), which computes a new subject from formula b and a new formula from formula c, enabling dynamic code execution and metaprogramming.
entries.description: 12 days of Nockmas: Evaluate, opcode 2

entries.title: Nockmas 2025: Day 4
entries.summary: Covers opcode 3 (Cell Check), the wut ? operator testing whether a noun is a cell (returns 0) or atom (returns 1), used for type discrimination.
entries.description: 12 days of Nockmas: Cell Check, opcode 3

entries.title: Nockmas 2025: Day 5
entries.summary: Covers opcode 4 (Increment), the lus + operator adding 1 to an atom, Nock's only arithmetic primitive from which all other arithmetic must be built.
entries.description: 12 days of Nockmas: Increment, opcode 4

entries.title: Nockmas 2025: Day 6
entries.summary: Covers opcode 5 (Equality Check), the tis = operator testing deep structural equality between two nouns, returning 0 if equal and 1 if not.
entries.description: 12 days of Nockmas: Equality Check, opcode 5

entries.title: Nockmas 2025: Day 7
entries.summary: Covers opcode 6 (Conditional), evaluating a test formula and returning either branch c for true (0) or d for false (1), implementing if-then-else logic.
entries.description: 12 days of Nockmas: Conditional, opcode 6

entries.title: Nockmas 2025: Day 8
entries.summary: Covers opcode 7 (Compose), evaluating b then using that result as the subject for c, implementing the pipe pattern for sequential function composition.
entries.description: 12 days of Nockmas: Compose, opcode 7

entries.title: Nockmas 2025: Day 9
entries.summary: Covers opcode 8 (Extend), pinning a new value to the head of the subject then evaluating the body, implementing variable binding where the new value is accessible at address 2.
entries.description: 12 days of Nockmas: Extend, opcode 8

entries.title: Nockmas 2025: Day 0
entries.summary: Introduces autocons, Nock's fundamental cell construction rule where formula structure becomes result structure, making cell construction free and enabling Nock's minimal design.
entries.description: 12 days of Nockmas: Intoducing autocons

entries.title: NockPU
entries.summary: Noah Kumin describes ~mopfel-winrux's NockPU hardware project, explaining how a stackless, tree-traversal-optimized processor differs from von Neumann architecture and could enable ultra-efficient Nock execution on bare metal.
entries.description: A light technical description of NockPU, a hardware system for running Nock

entries.title: Obelisk Beta Release
entries.summary: Obelisk brings a time-traveling relational database to Urbit. This post outlines urQL, why composability matters, what the beta makes available, and where to go for docs, roadmap, and the Hawk UI.
entries.description: A first look at Obelisk, a relational database for Urbit with time travel, composable queries, and no nulls.

entries.title: Olif and Urbit IDs
entries.summary: Technical specification for Olif identity format extension, explaining compatibility with existing Urbit ID standards and advantages for specific application use cases.
entries.description: An Olfactive rendering of Urbit Address Space

entries.title: On Christopher Alexander
entries.summary: Essay examining architect Christopher Alexander's influence on Urbit design philosophy, discussing patterns language, town building principles, and technology's relationship to human flourishing.
entries.description: An overview of his writing and relevance

entries.title: Pin the Face that Launches a Thousand Ships
entries.summary: ~nospex-larsut addresses standard hesitations to learning Hoon, arguing that Hoon's difficulty creates opportunity, Urbit's momentum is accelerating with new apps and hosting options, and learning now positions developers to benefit from early involvement.
entries.description: A guest post by ~nospex-larsut

entries.title: Designing a Permanent Personal Identity
entries.summary: Philip Monk explores Urbit's PKI design through an idea maze, explaining the tradeoffs between comets (plentiful, self-sovereign, impermanent), planets (scarce, permanent, self-sovereign), and moons (plentiful, permanent, planet-controlled) identities.
entries.description: A public key infrastructure (PKI) is a system for binding a set of keys to a name. Sometimes a small amount of metadata is included.

entries.title: Platform Decay, Decentralized Marketplaces, and Urbit
entries.summary: Nicholas Simmons argues e-commerce platforms decay toward exploitation, advocating decentralized marketplaces on Urbit with personal data ownership, neutral reputation systems, and calm commerce to restore fair vendor-customer relationships.
entries.description: Urbit is calm computing. Calm commerce follows naturally.

entries.title: Precepts
entries.summary: Formal statement of core principles including simplicity, durability, ownership, and user control, serving as decision framework for ongoing development work.
entries.description: Technical maxims that define Urbit's approach to engineering.

entries.title: Precepts: Discussion
entries.summary: Philip Monk justifies Urbit's precepts across general design, specific design, attitude, theory, text style, and real software, defending mechanical simplicity, state machines for permanence, and deterministic computing as essential to lasting software.
entries.description: The precepts aren’t arguments. We discuss and justify them here.

entries.title: Providers
entries.summary: Ecosystem announcement documenting third-party hosting services, comparing offerings, pricing, and features while maintaining standards for reliability and data portability.
entries.description: We’ve always assumed that providers would have to come into existence sooner or later. By the look of it, that time is now. Tlon and a few others have provider-like services in the works.

entries.title: Building Your DAO with Pseudonymous Reputation on Urbit
entries.summary: Anthony Arroyo explains how Combine DAO uses Urbit ID's pseudonymous reputation to select members based on past network behavior, solving DAO trust and accountability issues while maintaining anonymity and avoiding token-gated limitations.
entries.description: Using Urbit ID’s pseudonymous reputation model, DAO participants know Urbit ID holders’ past behavior before relying on them, and without sacrificing anonymity.

entries.title: The Gang Solves the Gas Crisis
entries.summary: Jonathan Paprocki explains Tlon's naive rollups layer-2 solution for reducing Urbit ID gas costs by 65x, detailing user experience changes, one-way migration, roller setup, and security guarantees that preserve ship sovereignty.
entries.description: How we're making Urbit ID affordable again

entries.title: Security and Continuity
entries.summary: Discussion of system resilience strategies including backup procedures, disaster recovery planning, and long-term data preservation for personal servers.
entries.description: An update on our primary infrastructure milestones for 2020.

entries.title: Ames Security Audit and the Future of the Protocol
entries.summary: Article examining security audit.
entries.description: Ames’ design has unparalleled potential to deter, mitigate, and recover from attacks, since every packet is authenticated and encrypted and backed by a stable, decentralized PKI.

entries.title: Simple, Durable, Yours
entries.summary: Philosophical framing of Urbit's design goals, explaining how simplicity enables durability and why user ownership is fundamental to trustworthy computing.
entries.description: We built Urbit from scratch to be a system that’s simple, durable, and yours. Everything that computing today is not — but should be.

entries.title: The Smart Home of the Future
entries.summary: This grant research explores smart home privacy needs, arguing Urbit's identity model should prioritize access control over legible reputation, enabling communal computing while respecting contextual integrity and household relationships.
entries.description: Homes are getting smarter. A smart home is no longer just a collection of smart devices but a superorganism of data-collecting objects. These people and devices who use and inhabit these homes form a complex socio-technical system. What is the future of the smart home and how will Urbit fit into it?

entries.title: Sovereign Intelligence
entries.summary: Vision statement for personal AI systems running on owned infrastructure, arguing for models that augment rather than replace human decision-making while protecting user privacy.
entries.description: Urbit offers a bicameral future for personal AI. One side stable. One side fluid. Each stronger with the other

entries.title: Stable Arvo
entries.summary: Milestone announcement for Arvo operating system stabilization, describing testing completion, API freeze, migration guides for existing applications, and long-term support commitments.
entries.description: This year we set out to get Arvo to a point that we can credibly call ‘stable.'

entries.title: State of Urbit
entries.summary: Comprehensive progress report covering technical development, community growth, application ecosystem expansion, and strategic priorities for ongoing platform evolution.
entries.description: A year in review

entries.title: Subssembly Hackathon 2024
entries.summary: Article examining community event.
entries.description: Use Login with Urbit ID in your app and win Urbit Stars

entries.title: The 100-Year Computer
entries.summary: Galen Wolfe-Pauly describes Urbit as a durable, reliable computer designed to never lose data, update itself continuously, and remain functional for decades without user intervention or ads.
entries.description: One way to think about Urbit: as a "100-year computer."

entries.title: The DAO as a Lesson in Decentralized Governance
entries.summary: Case study analysis of Ethereum DAO collapse, examining smart contract vulnerabilities, governance failures, and lessons applied to Urbit's constitutional framework.
entries.description: What's the right lesson for the decentralization community to learn from the collapse of the DAO?

entries.title: The Missing Middle
entries.summary: Analysis of gaps in current internet infrastructure, describing mid-tier services and protocols that don't fit binary choices between massive platforms and individual self-hosting.
entries.description: Urbit stars can facilitate a flexible continuum of community norms.

entries.title: The Shape of DAO Governance to Come
entries.summary: Forward-looking discussion on decentralized organizational evolution, predicting patterns of coordination and exploring how Urbit's identity layer enables new governance models.
entries.description: When building the Combine DAO, we conducted a survey of DAO governance and tooling and came to the conclusion that the many theoretical approaches to the problem of governance were tied to the implementation details of the DAO stack. Since we were building everything on Urbit—as opposed to through the typical combination of Solidity contracts, Web2 tools and Snapshot—we realized that we’d have to do some rethinking. A new approach for a new stack.

entries.title: The State of Landscape
entries.summary: Status report on Landscape interface development covering feature releases, usability improvements, performance optimizations, and roadmap for upcoming releases.
entries.description: An update on the state of Landscape and the Urbit network.

entries.title: The Understanding Urbit Podcast
entries.summary: Announcement of educational podcast series making Urbit concepts accessible through interviews, technical discussions, and community stories.
entries.description: An interview-based podcast series about the Urbit project, as told by those working on it.

entries.title: The Urbit Address Space
entries.summary: Technical specification for cryptographic identity system, explaining galaxy-planet-moon hierarchy, star minting economics, and how address space enables secure communication.
entries.description: An overview of Urbit's cryptographic address space.

entries.title: This Month in Urbit: April 2026
entries.summary: The first This Month in Urbit highlights April 2026 ecosystem experiments: Groundwire's Urbit MCP server, ~mopfel-winrux's %jars and %bide desks, %monsters, %mail, %sovnas, urbit-llmproxy, Talon, and other network updates.
entries.description: Experiments from around the Urbit ecosystem, including MCP, games, mail, local LLM routing, and mobile clients

entries.title: Tools of Our Own
entries.summary: Manifesto for building personal computing tools users control, arguing against platform dependency and describing benefits of self-hosted, open source software.
entries.description: What is a digital environment? What does it mean to shape your own digital environment?

entries.title: Toward a Frozen Operating System
entries.summary: Philosophical essay on software evolution, discussing when systems should stabilize versus continue changing, and Urbit's approach through Hoon's immutability guarantees.
entries.description: Is it possible to freeze an entire OS?

entries.title: Toward a New %clay
entries.summary: Curtis Yarvin proposes comprehensive redesign of Urbit's %clay revision control system, addressing storage models, Unix mounting, merge semantics, and security while inviting community contributions to improve the typed DVCS.
entries.description: Urbit's revision-control system, %clay, is itself due for a (medium-sized) revision!

entries.title: Urbit and Bitcoin
entries.summary: Analysis of cryptocurrency integration with Urbit, discussing Bitcoin philanthropy, Azimuth transactions on Ethereum, and synergies between different cryptographic asset systems.
entries.description: A sound money deserves a sound computer.

entries.title: Urbit and the Blockchain Wars
entries.summary: Curtis Yarvin explains Urbit's pragmatic decision to bootstrap on Ethereum despite criticisms, detailing governance migration options, security models, and why Urbit uses Ethereum as a land registry rather than endorsing the platform.
entries.description: A bit about the 'idea maze' of choosing to bootstrap from Ethereum.

entries.title: Urbit + Creator DAOs with Justin Murphy
entries.summary: Justin Murphy builds Other Life as a Creator DAO on Urbit, demonstrating how Straw enables democratic publishing, Ballot allows decentralized membership management, and Urbit's P2P real estate provides unprecedented durability and ownership.
entries.description: Creator DAOs are blank slates, new foundational cryptographic patterns just beginning to take shape. Justin Murphy thinks Urbit is the most obvious place to start building one.

entries.title: Urbit Is for Creators
entries.summary: Noah Kumin argues Urbit offers creators freedom from algorithmic manipulation and platform dependency, enabling direct peer-to-peer distribution, ownership of work, and sustainable communities through durable infrastructure.
entries.description: Urbit is for creators who are ready to wake up from this bad dream.

entries.title: Urbit for Normies
entries.summary: Accessibility guide explaining Urbit concepts in plain language, addressing common confusion points, and providing entry paths for non-technical users.
entries.description: A layperson’s guide to the coming new internet.

entries.title: Urbit Grants and mid-2019 Gifts
entries.summary: Article examining grants distribution.
entries.description: Announcing an upcoming Urbit grants program and star gifts for Mid-2019.

entries.title: Urbit is for Communities
entries.summary: Announcement of Urbit 0.8.0 release emphasizing community features, group governance tools, and applications supporting collective organization and collaboration.
entries.description: Urbit is for giving communities the tools to shape their own environments; for us all to feel a sense of life and self-directedness in the digital world.

entries.title: An Interview with UrbitHost
entries.summary: Article covering conversation and technical deep-dive, examining technical deep-dive and conversation.
entries.description: Interview with the founder of UrbitHost ~lavlyn-litmeg

entries.title: Using Urbit in 2023
entries.summary: Practical guide for new users covering initial setup, basic operations, landscape navigation, application recommendations, and community involvement opportunities.
entries.description: There are more ways to run Urbit than ever, and more options coming soon.

entries.title: The Value of Urbit Address Space (1 of 3)
entries.summary: Erik Newton and Galen Wolfe-Pauly explain what Urbit ID is, its history of distribution, and draw analogies to DNS, ISPs, CDNs, land, and dealer-operators to understand address space value beyond planet prices.
entries.description: An expansion of our position on Urbit's address space value.

entries.title: The Value of Urbit Address Space (2 of 3)
entries.summary: Erik Newton and Galen Wolfe-Pauly analyze Urbit address space value through scarcity, utility, liquidity, and network effect, examining trading patterns and explaining why Reed's law best describes Urbit's network effects.
entries.description: Scarcity, utility, liquidity, and network effect.

entries.title: The Value of Urbit Address Space (3 of 3)
entries.summary: Erik Newton and Galen Wolfe-Pauly explain lockups and spawning limits for Urbit address space, discuss distribution estimates, and clarify why privacy-by-design limits usage data collection despite the network explorer's development.

entries.title: What is Urbit For?
entries.summary: Galen Wolfe-Pauly outlines Urbit's vision for peer-to-peer computing, from self-hosted publishing and cryptographic identity today to data permanence, reputation systems, and IoT in the future.
entries.description: A vision of the Urbit-powered future.

entries.title: Why Hoon?
entries.summary: Ted Blackman explains Hoon's unique promise: enabling a purely functional operating system with hot code reload, typesafe metaprogramming, and universal serialization at full performance through jets and Nock.
entries.description: The promise of Urbit lies in its reimagination of the digital world using components that are as constrained and limited as possible.

entries.title: Why Urbit Probably Doesn't Need a Blockchain
entries.summary: Curtis Yarvin argues Urbit's PKI is a special case of a consensus ledger, explaining why the double-sell problem can be solved without full Nakamoto consensus through governance, routing topology, and patience.
entries.description: Urbit (probably) doesn't need a blockchain, because the Urbit address-space PKI is a special case of a consensus ledger.

entries.title: Your Last Computer
entries.summary: Vision essay describing computing experience where technology recedes into background, devices endure for decades, and users maintain permanent control over digital infrastructure.
entries.description: Your Urbit is a simpler computer, a quieter computer, a more private computer. We want it to feel predictable, safe, and reliable — things only a complete, sealed system can do. This, we hope, can get us a world where technology keeps us connected, but doesn’t dominate our lives.

entries.title: Urbit Austin
entries.summary: Curious about Urbit? Questions about Hoon syntax? Fed up with the current state of the internet? Join us to talk about the internet's future.

entries.title: Urbit Berlin
entries.summary: If you are sick of the current state of the web, of handing over your data and entire online presence to a handful of American corporations, of a web fueled with ads, then Urbit might be for you.

entries.title: DC Metro Urbit Meetup Group
entries.summary: This is a DC/Northern Virginia/Southern Maryland Urbit discussion group.

entries.title: Urbit Denver
entries.summary: While Urbit is the animating force behind this group, people of all interest levels & familiarity with the project - be they long-time users, or newcomers simply wondering what the heck Urbit is - are welcome & encouraged to attend these meet-ups. The conversations are lively and span a wide variety of topics, ranging from Urbit to philosophy, tech, crypto, the arts, health and well-being, and much more.

entries.title: Urbit Los Angeles
entries.summary: Join a group of like-minded decentralization enjoyers from the City of Angels to discuss all things Urbit and adjacent.

entries.title: Urbit London
entries.summary: Urbit London is a monthly, occasionally biweekly, meet-up series aiming to establish a long-lasting community within the London tech and cultural scene.

entries.title: Urbit New England
entries.summary: Meetings for Urbit users in the New England region. Expect free-flowing discussions. Hooning is always welcome.

entries.title: Urbit NY
entries.summary: We love experimenting with Urbit & tinkering with Arvo, Hoon, & Nock

entries.title: Urbit Paris
entries.summary: Join us for our monthly Urbit meetups in Paris!

entries.title: Urbit SF
entries.summary: Want to own and control the server side? Your digital identity, data and computation?

entries.title: Urbit Tokyo
entries.summary: A place for osmosis between those working on, or interested in Urbit (or adjacent projects). Let’s build something, or at the very least - we can procrastinate together in style.

entries.title: Ecosystem
entries.summary: Selected organizations and external coverage from the wider Urbit ecosystem.
entries.description: Selected organizations and coverage from the wider Urbit ecosystem.

entries.title: Alphabet
entries.summary: Alphabet is a peer-to-peer prediction market built on Urbit. It leverages the network’s decentralized structure and built-in reputation system for fundamental breakthroughs in the design of a betting platform. Because Alphabet is on Urbit, users can make uncensorable, pseudonymous bets against other users.
entries.description: %alphabet is a prediction market built on Urbit.

entries.title: Chess
entries.summary: “The ability to play chess is the sign of a gentleman. The ability to play chess well is the sign of a wasted life.” —Paul Morphy, Grandmaster
entries.description: Fully decentralized, peer-to-peer Chess app for Urbit

entries.title: Face
entries.summary: On Urbit, you don’t have to share your photos with just anybody. Upload a photo of yourself and then curate who you’ll allow to see it using Pals. Just make sure to upload your most dapper photo and voila. Now when someone spies you out of the corner of their eye at the next great Urbit event, they’ll know just who they’re talking to.
entries.description: It’s time to put a face to that name

entries.title: Hits
entries.summary: One challenge decentralized networks face is that there’s no central place to discover new software or content. The Urbit Foundation’s Urbit Labs team has designed an app, Hits, to solve this problem.
entries.description: The ultimate way to discover Urbit apps

entries.title: Pals
entries.summary: What’s the internet without friends?
entries.description: Friendlist for peer discovery

entries.title: Quorum
entries.summary: Quorum is a Stack Overflow/Quora style application that allows individuals to submit and vote on questions and answers in threads. To join the Urbit Foundation's [battery payload] board, when prompted to add a board, enter ~dister-dozzod-lapdeg as the provider, and battery-payload as the board.
entries.description: A choral explanations app for Urbit

entries.title: Radio
entries.summary: Remember the days of turning on the radio in a car full of friends and jamming out to whatever happens to be on the dial? Those days don’t have to be dead and gone. Radio allows you to browse the YouTube channels other Urbit users are watching and chat with them in real-time. It takes a public good (YouTube) and turns it into a digitally networked clubhouse. Rock on!
entries.description: an app for urbit disc jockeys

entries.title: Rumors
entries.summary: Psst. Did you hear the latest? Rumors is an app so elegant and simple that it could only exist on Urbit. It is an anonymous message board but with an Urbit twist: you’ll only see messages from your pals (Urbit users you’ve added on the %pals app) and pals of pals.
entries.description: Anonymous gossip from friends of friends

entries.title: Tlon
entries.summary: Whether you're a team, a publication, or a group of friends, Tlon is an app that provides a few simple basics for communities to shape into something unique to their needs. Get comfortable and compute in peace, an internet of intimate connection.
entries.description: Peer-to-peer collaboration for communities of all shapes and sizes

entries.title: Trill
entries.summary: Trill is a micro-blogging platform on Urbit. It’s a simple proposition with some highly interesting consequences. For one thing, the app is composable with other apps. So you can import your list of Urbit pals. It also means that your feed is not being fine-tuned by a massive corporation trying to nudge you in the direction of advertisements. This way you can check up on what your friends are thinking, and avoid the spammers, ads, and copypasta content. So let those posts sing.
entries.description: Urbit Native Microblog

entries.title: Turf
entries.summary: Turf is Urbit's very own 2D pixel-art metaverse, where anyone can explore and add to the burgeoning network of customizable digital spaces, or "turfs." Turf blurs the line between art, programming and gaming in a way that you'll only find on Urbit.
entries.description: decentralized, self-sovereign 2D metaverse, powered by Urbit, it's free real estate

entries.title: Uniswap
entries.summary: Self-hosted uniswap front-end made by Laconic
entries.description: Self-hosted Uniswap interface

entries.title: Urbitswap
entries.summary: Urbitswap is currently an app for trading Urbit NFTs. It is currently being developed in conjunction with VentureClub to enable legally compliant peer trading of securities. Avoid centralized front-ends and choke points. Trade on Urbit.
entries.description: A peer-to-peer NFT trading platform native to Urbit

entries.title: Crazy Wisdom
entries.summary: The world is currently undergoing a meaning crisis. Many of the age-old dogmas, traditions, and ideologies we once drew wisdom from have shown deep flaws. Where do we go from here? Join Stewart Alsop, as he and his guests share the many lessons they’ve learned exploring this question, and as they pierce through the facade of ideology and instead seek to interact with experience as it actually is.
entries.description: Find meaning through their episodes with a high frequency of Urbit conversations.

entries.title: Epicenter
entries.summary: Epicenter brings you in-depth conversations about the technical, economic and social implications of cryptocurrencies and blockchain technologies. Every week, we interview business leaders, engineers academics and entrepreneurs, and bring you a diverse spectrum of opinions and points of view. Epicenter is hosted by Sebastien Couture, Brian Fabian Crain, Friederike Ernst, Meher Roy and Felix Lutsch. Since 2014, our episodes have been downloaded over 8 million times.
entries.description: The blockchain & web3 podcast co-hosted by Urbit Foundation board member Brian Crain.

entries.title: Other Life
entries.summary: "The third important way of being militant is militancy as bearing witness by one’s life in the form of a style of existence. This style of existence specific to revolutionary militantism, and ensuring that one’s life bears witness, breaks, and has to break with the conventions, habits, and values of society. And it must manifest directly, by its visible form, its constant practice, and its immediate existence, the concrete possibility and the evident value of an other life, which is the true life." —Michel Foucault
entries.description: A podcast by Urbit enthousiast Justin Murphy.

entries.title: Red Horizon Podcast
entries.summary: Red Horizon is designed by Chorus One. They work to expand the freedom and sovereignty that human beings experience in their digital lives. In their Podcast ~tiller-tolbus explore the Urbit ecosystem and it's developers through engaging conversations.
entries.description: Dive into the world of Urbit with Red Horizon's ~tiller-tolbus

entries.title: The Stack
entries.summary: The Sovereign Stack is a podcast focused on exploring individual sovereignty. Urbit and Reserve give people tools to defend their sovereignty.
entries.description: Individual Sovereignty and Urbit is what drives the Stack podcast.

entries.title: Understanding Urbit
entries.summary: As a highly secure personal server, Urbit aims to deliver on many of the ideas pioneered by the Cypherpunks. After nearly 20 years in development the platform has begun a phased launch. Urbit gives us persistent digital identity, a new benchmark for secure computing, and maybe even an open source response to modern social computing platforms like WeChat and Kakaotalk.
entries.description: A timeless & comprehensive guide through Urbit's design ideas from 2020.

entries.title: ZeroK
entries.summary: Zero K brings you behind the scenes salvos from the people building Urbit, the decentralized operating system. Join us as we cover the latest news from the Urbit Foundation and interview the contributors making Urbit real.
entries.description: The Urbit Foundation's official podcast. Hosted by ~poldec-tonteg

entries.title: Zorp
entries.summary: Zorp has built a secure-by-design zero-knowledge virtual machine (zkVM) with a uniquely minimal software supply chain which utilizes Nock as its instruction set. Nock, Urbit’s low-level language, is a famously simple instruction set. That property aligns itself well with what you might create if you were designing a language from first principles to do zero-knowledge proofs. Zorp’s zkVM could unlock new possibilities for crypto and more. Check back at Zorp’s website and Twitter for more info.
entries.description: We assure critical computation using zero-knowledge proofs

entries.title: ~tocwex.syndicate
entries.summary: Syndicates are portable digital organizations designed for 'network tribes' that value decentralization, personal sovereignty, and collective innovation. Through a combination of onchain digital assets and tools, along with off-chain software running over a peer-to-peer and end-to-end encrypted network, Syndicates are able to operate as self-sovereign socioeconomic networks, both onchain and off-chain. ~tocwex.syndicate is a practice in recursive creation of syndicate tooling.
entries.description: recursively building digital syndicates

entries.title: Tlon
entries.summary: Tlon Corporation is the first developer of Urbit, and their work continues to maintain core infrastructure development in addition to designing products for communities on the network. Tlon’s primary products are Hosting, cloud storage for your urbit, Landscape, an operating system and interface, and Groups, a communication suite.
entries.description: The first developer of Urbit. Their work continues to maintain core infrastructure development in addition to designing products for communities on the network.

entries.title: Sortug Development
entries.summary: Sortug is a DAO focused on software development and promotion of Urbit to a global audience. We are focused on creating and distributing Urbit applications and on integrating Urbit in Earth business and communities through innovative gatekeeping ideas.
entries.description: Sortug is an Urbit development DAO based on Earth Time UTC+08:00.

entries.title: Octu Ventures
entries.summary: Octu Ventures is a member-driven venture DAO created to invest in teams building on Urbit. We intend to leverage our long history in the Urbit ecosystem, relationships with key builders and community leaders, and analysis of Urbit’s place in the current technological, financial, and geopolitical landscape in order to help Urbit infrastructure companies grow to the world-changing size they deserve to.
entries.description: A member-driven venture DAO investing in teams building on urbit

entries.title: Native Planet
entries.summary: Native Planet builds Urbit centric hardware and software that simplifies sovereign self hosting and ship management. Native Planet's open source software enables users to turn nearly any computer into an Urbit dedicated server in minutes.
entries.description: Native Planet builds Urbit-centric hardware and software that simplifies sovereign self hosting and ship management.

entries.description: The Mars Review of Books is a magazine in print and on Urbit which combines ruthlessly intelligent and fearless sense-making on subjects of global importance with today’s most stylish belletristic writing on contemporary arts and culture.

Find Labyrinth DAO on Twitter or connect at labyrinthdao.io to get involved.

entries.title: Get On The Network
entries.summary: Entry points for getting on Urbit, including hosted options and self-hosting resources.

entries.title: Homepage
entries.summary: Overview of Urbit with links to getting started, running a ship, and the broader ecosystem.

entries.title: Overview
entries.summary: Overview section navigation hub providing entry points to two main learning paths: Urbit Explained for conceptual foundations and technical architecture, and Running Urbit for practical setup, hosting, and usage guides.
entries.description: Overview section navigation hub providing entry points to two main learning paths: Urbit Explained for conceptual foundations and technical architecture, and Running Urbit for practical setup, hosting, and usage guides.

entries.title: Running Urbit
entries.summary: Introduction to running Urbit covering the two required steps of acquiring an Urbit ID and booting Urbit OS, with examples of deployment options ranging from galaxy-level infrastructure to comets on local laptops.
entries.description: A comprehensive guide to getting started with Urbit

entries.title: Common Commands
entries.summary: Reference guide for essential dojo commands including ship management operations, application installation and status checking, azimuth state verification, memory optimization, moon identity creation, and shutdown procedures.
entries.description: Essential commands for using Urbit

entries.title: Add and remove applications
entries.summary: Learn the dojo commands |install, |nuke, and |uninstall to manage your urbit's application lifecycle, from adding new apps to removing all associated state.
entries.description: 3rd-party software distribution enables any urbit node to distribute software to the network, these commands help you manage your installed apps.

entries.title: Check and reduce memory usage
entries.summary: Use |mass, |meld, and |pack commands to monitor and compress your urbit's memory, reducing loom usage by up to 50% on resource-constrained systems.
entries.description: Monitor and optimize your urbit's memory consumption

entries.title: Check application info and status
entries.summary: The +vats command displays metadata for all apps or a specific app, showing cz hash, status, publishing ship, and pending updates for troubleshooting.
entries.description: Running `+vats` will output the state of your apps and related metadata that can help with troubleshooting

entries.title: Check your current sponsor
entries.summary: The +sponsor command reveals your ship's current sponsor @p, useful for diagnosing connectivity issues when a ship in your sponsorship chain is offline.
entries.description: Understanding your networking sponsor can help with troubleshooting connectivity issues

entries.title: Checking and fixing Azimuth state
entries.summary: Check Ethereum block sync with +azimuth/block and use -azimuth-load to fetch snapshots if your ship's Azimuth state is out of sync with the blockchain.
entries.description: Monitor and repair your PKI state synchronization

entries.title: Create a moon identity
entries.summary: Use |moon to generate moon identities linked to your planet, with additional commands like |moon-breach for factory resets and |moon-cycle-keys to update cryptographic keys.
entries.description: Spawn subordinate identities tied to your planet

entries.title: Directly contact another urbit
entries.summary: The |hi command sends a peer-to-peer ping to another Urbit ship with an optional message, the most basic p2p messaging affordance in the dojo.
entries.description: Urbit's most basic messaging protocol can send a quick hi in the dojo

entries.title: Docking your urbit
entries.summary: Use the dock command to copy the Vere runtime binary into your pier, making it self-contained with a specific runtime version for portable deployment.
entries.description: Install a specific runtime version into your pier

entries.title: Get access code
entries.summary: Use the +code command to retrieve your eight-syllable access code for logging into web interfaces and mobile clients, distinct from your master ticket.
entries.description: A secret code for remote access to your instance of Urbit OS

entries.title: Get the Urbit runtime
entries.summary: Install the Urbit runtime with the guided get-runtime.sh helper or use direct per-platform commands for macOS and Linux.
entries.description: Download the runtime binary for your host platform

entries.title: Reduce your pier size
entries.summary: Use Vere's roll and chop commands to create new epochs and truncate event logs, reducing pier size from hundreds of GB to under 10GB on long-running ships.
entries.description: Compress and clean up your urbit's disk usage

entries.title: Select available loom size
entries.summary: Use the --loom flag with a power-of-two exponent to set memory allocation: 30=1GB, 31=2GB (default), 32=4GB, or 33=8GB based on system resources.
entries.description: Configure memory allocation for your urbit

entries.title: Shut down your urbit
entries.summary: Use |exit or Ctrl-D from dojo to gracefully shut down your urbit, though Urbit's solid-state design allows straightforward recovery from power loss.
entries.description: Gracefully stop your urbit instance

entries.title: Start and stop applications on your urbit
entries.summary: Manage applications with |start, |suspend, |pause, and |revive commands to control specific agents, suspend desks, prevent updates, or restart suspended agents.
entries.description: Control application lifecycles with dojo commands

entries.title: Restart up your urbit after initial boot
entries.summary: Boot your urbit with ./urbit /path/to/pier, or use the auto-docked .run script after initial boot for version-pinned, standalone execution.
entries.description: Restarting your urbit after intial boot is straightforward and doesn't require additional cryptographic secrets

entries.title: Update Commands For Your Urbit
entries.summary: Use |bump to apply kernel updates and suspend incompatible apps, or |ota to change your Over-The-Air update provider if your sponsor is derelict.
entries.description: Your urbit is generally auto-updating, but in the event of an incompatible application or a kernel update that would conflict with existing apps, you may need to decide which software to run

entries.title: Update your urbit runtime
entries.summary: Use ./urbit next /path/to/pier to automatically check for and install Vere runtime updates with the latest performance improvements and bug fixes.
entries.description: Keep your vere binary up to date

entries.title: Get Urbit ID
entries.summary: Methods for obtaining an Urbit ID including purchasing Layer 1 Azimuth identities on NFT marketplaces, acquiring Layer 2 identities from vendors using Bitcoin or fiat, and receiving invitations from existing users.
entries.description: Learn how to acquire your own Urbit identity

entries.title: Azimuth-based Urbit IDs
entries.summary: Azimuth identities are Urbit addresses registered as NFTs on Ethereum (Layer 1 or Layer 2 rollup), purchasable via marketplaces like OpenSea or from third-party sellers.
entries.description: Azimuth identities are cryptographically owned Urbit address space on the Ethereum blockchain

entries.title: Buy an Urbit ID
entries.summary: Acquire a self-sovereign Urbit ID via Layer 1 NFT marketplaces like OpenSea or Layer 2 providers accepting Bitcoin or credit cards, with planets (4-syllable @p) recommended for new users.
entries.description: Learn how to acquire your own self-sovereign digital identity

entries.title: Groundwire-based Urbit IDs
entries.summary: Groundwire is a Bitcoin-based Urbit identity system using ordinals for independent self-issuance with L1 Bitcoin's spam resistance.
entries.description: Groundwire identities are cryptographically owned Urbit address space on the Bitcoin blockchain.

entries.title: Self-custody your Urbit ID
entries.summary: Self custody means controlling your cryptographic assets directly through master ticket wallets, software wallets like MetaMask, or hardware wallets like Ledger or Trezor.
entries.description: As a cryptographic asset, there are many ways to control and secure your Urbit ID

entries.title: Urbit master ticket wallets
entries.summary: Master ticket wallets shard cryptographic permissions for Urbit IDs like a brainwallet, with support in Bridge alongside standard wallets and hardware wallets.
entries.description: Master ticket wallets are an easy and secure way for managing ownership of your Urbit ID

entries.title: Glossary
entries.summary: Terminology reference guide for Urbit covering ships, piers, galaxies, stars, planets, moons, comets, and other domain-specific vocabulary used throughout Urbit documentation and community discussions.
entries.description: Common terms and concepts in Urbit

entries.title: Hosting Providers
entries.summary: Third-party managed hosting services for Urbit ships that handle technical infrastructure management, with coverage of providers like Tlon, incentives for hosts, and tradeoffs compared to self-hosting.
entries.description: Explore hosting options for your Urbit

entries.title: Tlon hosting services
entries.summary: Tlon provides free Layer 2 hosting with co-custody master tickets, quick onboarding via invite links, and email-based account creation without crypto wallet requirements.
entries.description: Tlon Corporation is the preeminent hosting provider which provides free and seamless onboarding to the Urbit network

entries.title: Shortfalls of hosting providers
entries.summary: Hosting providers must keep your urbit unencrypted at runtime, limiting privacy, and offer narrower interaction options like web interfaces rather than full SSH access.
entries.description: Hosting providers are designed to be scalable, not bespoke, operations.

entries.title: Urbit ID incentives for hosts
entries.summary: Cryptographic ID ownership enforces honest hosting by letting users override malevolent hosts at any time, shifting the principal-agent relationship back to user control.
entries.description: Cryptographic ownership of Urbit ID helps enforce honest operation of Urbit OS by hosting providers

entries.title: Why use a hosting provider?
entries.summary: Hosting providers exist because Urbit isn't quite as easy as 'caring for a cactus' yet, and because networks effects matter for users who aren't inclined to self-host.
entries.description: Urbit is a personal server, yet there are still service providers who will host it for you

entries.title: Run urbit in a virtual server
entries.summary: Run Urbit on cloud providers like AWS, Linode, or Digital Ocean for reliable uptime, with minimum requirements of 1 vCPU, 2-4GB RAM, and 10-40GB storage.
entries.description: Urbit runs seamlessly in any cloud server or datacenter you may already be familiar with

entries.title: Run urbit locally
entries.summary: Run Urbit on your local laptop or desktop for peer-to-peer networking access, though public domain access requires additional configuration beyond local hosting.
entries.description: Quickly and easily run urbit on your laptop, or home desktop computer

entries.title: Run Urbit Using Groundseg
entries.summary: Groundseg provides a GUI for managing Urbit containers via Docker, with features for loom adjustment, event log truncation, and automatic maintenance routines.
entries.description: Groundseg is free and open-source software for running urbits, developed by Native Planet

entries.title: Run urbit using Native Planet hardware
entries.summary: Native Planet sells purpose-built hardware with ColonyOS and Groundseg preloaded, offering managed hosting features like Startram DNS while maintaining local sovereignty.
entries.description: Placeholder description

entries.title: Urbit as overlay OS
entries.summary: Urbit OS runs as an overlay OS via the Vere runtime on any Unix host, allowing your entire 'pier' to be portable across hardware like a zipped virtual machine.
entries.description: Urbit OS is a personal server operating system that runs on any Unix box as a self-contained virtual machine

entries.summary: Support resources for Urbit users encountering issues or with questions, providing links to community forums and contact points for assistance from the Urbit community and core developers.

entries.title: Partnerships and outreach
entries.summary: Contact the Urbit Foundation partnerships team for collaborations, events, integrations, and other outreach opportunities.

entries.title: Off-network support channels
entries.summary: Get off-network support for runtime issues via support@urbit.org and GitHub, or Tlon Messenger issues via support@tlon.io when unable to reach the network directly.
entries.description: Not able to get onto the network at all? Here are some off-network channels for getting support

entries.title: On-network support channels
entries.summary: Join Tlon Messenger groups like Tlon Local, battery payload, Hooniverse, Urbit Community, and Urbit Foundation for support with hardware, development, Hoon, and general help.
entries.description: Need help with something? Give a shout in one of these groups and someone will give you a hand

entries.title: Urbit Explained
entries.summary: Introduction to Urbit as an approach to building durable computing infrastructure, explaining the motivation for a long-lasting software stack and the two-part structure of identity and operating system. Covers Urbit ID and Urbit OS as core components.
entries.description: Urbit is a practice in building a forever computer

entries.title: Beyond
entries.summary: Overview of Urbit's ecosystem and development goals, including the Urbit Foundation, private companies like Tlon and Native Planet, and technical contributions such as Named Data Networking. Explains how each component can be used independently and resources for contributors.
entries.description: Building towards a decentralized future for a distributed network

entries.title: Urbit ID
entries.summary: Urbit ID is the naming and addressing system for the network, with five tiers from galaxies to comets. Each identity provides cryptographic authentication and contact info via the Azimuth contracts on Ethereum.
entries.description: A decentralized identity system for the Urbit network

entries.title: Urbit OS
entries.summary: Urbit OS is an overlay system built on the Nock virtual machine, with a functional language, kernel, and core modules like filesystem and networking. It keeps state as a pure function of its event history.
entries.description: A new operating system designed to give individuals control of their digital lives

entries.title: Bounties
entries.summary: Bounties are opportunities to work on Urbit in exchange for address space or other funding. Bounties are perfect if you want inspiration or direction on what's needed. They are posted by the Urbit Foundation, or other ecosystem organizations.
entries.description: Pick up a work opportunity in the Urbit ecosystem
